text: The video shows a robotic-assisted abdominal perineal resection, including extended total mesorectal excision (TMA) and pelvic lymph node dissection.
Our patient is a thirty seven year old gentleman with rectal neuroendocrine carcinoma with the involvement of the left lateral mesorectal fascia by nodes.
Patient was given fifty gray in twenty five fractions with five cycles of cisplatin and etoposide.
The robotic surgical system, Darwin GXI, is docked at the left lower abdomen, preparing for the abdominoperineal resection procedure.
The surgical setup includes standard robotic ports along with two assistant ports, facilitating access for the robotic instruments and surgical team.
The surgeon initiates dissection by creating a plane below the inferior mesenteric vein, proceeding to dissect along the mesocolic fascia from medial to lateral.
The surgeon tents up the rectal mesentery and scores the peritoneum below the inferior mesenteric artery, preparing for further dissection.
The surgeon continues the dissection below the inferior mesenteric artery, progressing from medial to lateral along the mesocolic fascia using robotic instruments.
The surgeon exposes the root of the inferior mesenteric artery, applies clips to secure it, and then divides the artery using surgical instruments.
The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection along the mesocolic fascia, progressing towards the lateral peritoneal attachment.
The surgeon continues the dissection in the pelvis, completing the posterior dissection down to the pelvic floor.
The surgeon performs a lateral peritoneal cut to mobilize the descending colon, separating it from the lateral attachments.
The surgeon begins the pelvic node dissection by scoring the peritoneum over the iliac vessels, exposing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon dissects and medializes the ureters, carefully defining the vesico-hypogastric fascia while ensuring it remains intact.
The surgeon dissects nodal tissue along the external iliac vessels, followed by dissection along the internal iliac vessels, carefully separating the nodal tissue from the surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies and divides the distal end of the obturator artery within the pelvic region, completing the dissection around this vascular structure.
The surgeon continues the dissection, revealing the obturator nerve and the obturator vein within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies and divides the distal end of the obturator vein, completing the dissection of this vascular structure.
The surgeon carefully dissects the nodal tissue along the obturator nerve, ensuring precise separation from surrounding structures.
The surgeon carefully avoids injury to the obturator nerve during the dissection process.
The surgeon identifies the obliterated umbilical artery, applies clips to secure it, and then divides the artery, completing the dissection of this structure.
The surgeon identifies and divides the superior vesicle artery, following the previous dissection of the obturator nerve and vein.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the remaining nodal tissue along the internyalic vessels, carefully separating it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies the distal end of the obliterated umbilical artery, clips it, and divides it, completing the dissection of this vessel.
The surgeon clears the remaining nodal tissue along the obturator nerve, then clips and divides the obturator vein at its origin.
The surgeon continues the dissection along the internal iliac vessels, progressing downward until the lumbosacral trunk is visually identified.
The surgeon clips and divides the internal pudendal vessels, completing the dissection in the pelvic region.
The surgeon identifies the sacrospinous ligament, which serves as a landmark for locating the ischial spine during the dissection.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection of the rectum while preserving the vasico hypogastric fascia to protect the hypogastric nerve. During the dissection, the middle rectal vessels are encountered, clipped, and divided.
The surgeon continues dissection along the internal iliac vessels, clearing nodal tissue along the internal pudendal vessels. The internal pudendal vessel is clipped and divided.
The surgeon makes an anterior peritoneal cut and continues the anterior dissection, carefully preserving the seminal vesicles visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon cuts the sacrospinous ligament at its origin from the ischial spine due to mesorectal fascia involvement by the node at the ischial spine.
The surgeon cuts the right levator ani muscle, ensuring adequate soft tissue margins are maintained during the dissection.
The surgeon uses an endo stapler to divide the specimen proximally, completing the separation of the tissue.
The surgeon completes the remaining part of the abdominoperineal resection perineally, excising the specimen through the perineum.
The video highlights the attachment of the tissue to the sacrospinous ligament, indicated by an arrow.
The final histopathology revealed large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma with negative proximal distal and circumferential resection margins.
